shortcut: An autonomous sentence (autonome zin / hoofdzin) can stand alone. A non-autonomous sentence (niet-autonome zin / bijzin) depends on another clause AND sends its verb to the end.
explanation: Belgian Dutch teachers split every clause into two types. An autonomous sentence (also called hoofdzin) makes sense on its own — it has a subject, an action, and information, in the V2 order you already know. A non-autonomous sentence (also called bijzin) is glued onto a main clause by a conjunction (omdat, dat, als, wanneer, …) and follows a completely different rule: the conjugated verb is kicked to the END. Once you can spot which type a clause is, the word-order question answers itself.

examples: Test: read the clause aloud on its own. If it makes complete sense and feels finished, it's autonomous.
Het regent. ← stands alone, makes sense → autonomous
Jan studeert Nederlands. ← stands alone, makes sense → autonomous
...omdat ik moe ben. ← does NOT stand alone (the 'omdat' demands a main clause to attach to) → non-autonomous
An autonomous sentence is a complete unit of meaning. A non-autonomous sentence is a dependent fragment that needs a host.

patterns.rule: Two Dutch terms for the same thing — autonome zin = hoofdzin
examples: autonome zin (autonomous sentence) = hoofdzin (main sentence) = can stand alone
niet-autonome zin (non-autonomous sentence) = bijzin (side sentence) = needs a host
Both pairs of terms mean the same thing. CVO Scala uses 'autonome zin / niet-autonome zin'. Textbooks often use 'hoofdzin / bijzin'. Same concept, different labels.

patterns.rule: Standard autonomous-sentence structure — Subject · Actie · Informatie
examples: An autonomous sentence has three roles, always in V2 order: someone (subject) does something (actie / verb) about/at/with something (informatie).

examples.caption: 'Mijn zus woont in Engeland.' — more informatie
'Het regent vandaag hard.' — no real subject, weather pattern

examples: ↳ 'Actie' is the everyday Belgian-Dutch label your teacher will use. It's the same thing as 'werkwoord' (verb) or 'persoonsvorm' (conjugated verb).

patterns.rule: Non-autonomous sentences — the verb kicks to the END
examples: A non-autonomous sentence opens with a subordinating conjunction (omdat, dat, als, wanneer, terwijl, hoewel, …). That conjunction is the trigger: the conjugated verb moves to the very END of the clause.

examples.caption: 'omdat ik moe ben.' — the verb 'ben' is at the END (not at position 2)
Same in English — but English keeps the verb in normal order

examples: ↳ This is the most important Dutch-vs-English difference at this level. English: 'because I AM tired.' Dutch: 'omdat ik moe BEN.' The verb position is reversed.

patterns.rule: Putting them together — hoofdzin + bijzin in real sentences
examples: Most natural Dutch combines an autonomous sentence (hoofdzin) with a non-autonomous one (bijzin), glued by a conjunction.

examples.caption: 'Ik ga naar huis omdat ik moe ben.' — hoofdzin + bijzin
'Hij zegt dat hij komt.' — the hoofdzin is just 'Hij zegt', the rest is a bijzin

examples: ↳ Notice how each half plays by its own rules: the main clause keeps V2 (verb at position 2), the subclause sends its verb to the END.

patterns.rule: Two verbs in a bijzin — both at the end, modal first, infinitive last
examples: When a non-autonomous sentence has TWO verbs (modal + infinitive), they both go to the end, in that order.

examples.caption: '…omdat ik koffie wil drinken.' — modal 'wil' before infinitive 'drinken', both at end
'…dat hij niet kan komen.' — negation slots before the verb cluster

patterns.rule: Yes/no questions are autonomous too — but with verb leading
examples: A yes/no question is still an autonomous sentence — it can stand alone. The structure differs: position 1 is empty, the verb leads, subject follows.

examples.caption: 'Werk jij vandaag?' — autonomous, verb-led
examples.note: Pos 1 is empty in yes/no questions. The verb is still in slot 2 of the V2 rule — there's just nothing in slot 1. ⚠ -t DROPPED before 'jij' (statement would be 'jij werkt').

patterns.rule: Conjunction quick-spot guide — does this make a clause non-autonomous?
examples: Coordinating conjunctions (en, maar, of, want, dus) glue two AUTONOMOUS sentences together. Each half keeps its V2 order.
Subordinating conjunctions (dat, omdat, als, wanneer, terwijl, hoewel, …) introduce a NON-AUTONOMOUS clause. The verb kicks to the end.

Belgian-Dutch trap: 'want' (coordinating, both clauses autonomous) and 'omdat' (subordinating, the omdat-clause is non-autonomous) both mean 'because' — but follow different rules. See [[conjunctions]] for the full pair.
Compare:

examples.caption: WANT (coordinating) — both halves are autonomous, both keep V2
OMDAT (subordinating) — the omdat-half is non-autonomous, verb at end

memory: 🧠 Two questions to answer for any Dutch clause: (1) Is it autonomous (can it stand alone)? — if yes, use V2 (verb at position 2). (2) Did a subordinating conjunction (omdat, dat, als, wanneer, …) trigger this clause? — if yes, verb kicks to the END. The conjunction is the visible signal of which rule to apply.

examples.caption: Hoofdzin + bijzin — main verb at pos 2, sub verb at END
Two-verb bijzin — modal then infinitive, both at end

practice.nl: Is dit een autonome zin: 'Jan studeert Nederlands'?
practice.en: Is this an autonomous sentence: 'Jan studeert Nederlands'?

practice.nl: Ja — het kan alleen staan. Subject ('Jan') + actie ('studeert') + informatie ('Nederlands'). Klassieke V2.
practice.en: Yes — it can stand alone. Subject + action + information. Classic V2.

practice.nl: Niet autonoom — 'omdat' eist een hoofdzin. En het werkwoord 'ben' staat aan het einde, niet op positie 2.
practice.en: Not autonomous — 'omdat' demands a main clause. And the verb 'ben' is at the END, not at position 2.

practice.nl: Wat is het verschil tussen 'want' en 'omdat'?
Beide betekenen 'because'. Maar 'want' is nevenschikkend — beide helften zijn autonoom. 'Omdat' is onderschikkend — de omdat-helft is niet autonoom en het werkwoord gaat naar het einde.
practice.en: Both mean 'because'. But 'want' is coordinating — both halves are autonomous. 'Omdat' is subordinating — the omdat-half is non-autonomous and the verb goes to the end.

practice.nl: Schrijf één hoofdzin en één bijzin samen.
practice.en: Write one main clause and one subclause together.

practice.nl: Ik leer Nederlands omdat ik in België woon. Hoofdzin: 'Ik leer Nederlands' (V2). Bijzin: 'omdat ik in België woon' (werkwoord 'woon' aan het einde).
practice.en: I learn Dutch because I live in Belgium. Main clause: V2. Subclause: verb at the end.
